}

docs.title: Consumers
docs.text: Modules built with the builder include: - logos-accounts-module — accounts/wallet operations (universal interface) - logos-capability-module — authorization token management - logos-chat-module — chat messaging - logos-package-manager-module — local package management - logos-waku-module — Waku network communication - logos-test-modules — test suite modules - All UI modules (logos-accounts-ui, logos-chat-ui, etc.)

docs.title: Supported Platforms
docs.text:
• macOS (aarch64-darwin, x86_64-darwin)
  • Linux (aarch64-linux, x86_64-linux)

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
• LogosModule.cmake lives in logos-plugin-qt, not in this repository — changing CMake behavior requires modifying the backend
  • Header generation requires building the full plugin first (reflection via QPluginLoader) — this will be addressed by LIDL-based generation
  • ui_qml modules do not produce include outputs (no C++ API to generate wrappers for)
  • Transitive dependency collection (collectAllModuleDeps) requires all transitive deps to have packages.lgx or be bundleable via nix-bundle-lgx
  • External library builds are always from-source — no binary cache support for external libs

docs.title: Overall Description
docs.text:
The Logos Module Builder is a shared build library that provides a declarative, configuration-driven pipeline for building Logos modules. It takes a module's source code and a metadata.json configuration file as input and produces ready-to-use plugin binaries, generated SDK headers, distributable packages, and development environments as output.

The builder is designed to: - Reduce module build configuration from ~600 lines across 5 files to ~70 lines across 2-3 files - Provide a single source of truth (metadata.json) for both build-time configuration and runtime plugin metadata - Abstract away the plugin compilation backend so modules are not permanently coupled to a specific technology (e.g., Qt) - Automatically resolve module dependencies, build external C/C++ libraries, generate type-safe SDK wrappers, and produce distributable packages - Support multiple module types (core logic, C++ UI, QML UI) through a unified configuration schema

docs.title: Definitions & Acronyms
docs.text: Term Definition Module An independently developed plugin for the Logos platform, built as a shared library (.so/.dylib) Builder The logos-module-builder Nix flake library — the subject of this document Backend A pluggable compilation strategy (e.g., Qt plugin, future CBOR standalone) that the builder delegates to metadata.json The declarative configuration file that describes a module's identity, dependencies, and build settings Plugin The compiled shared library output of a module build (e.g., my_module_plugin.so) LGX Logos package format — a gzip tar archive containing platform-specific module variants for distribution SDK Headers Generated C++ wrapper classes that provide type-safe access to a module's methods from other modules External Library A third-party C/C++ library (e.g., a Go CGo static library) that a module depends on and the builder compiles from source Standalone App A host application (logos-standalone-app) that can load and run a single UI module for development and testing Flake Input A Nix flake dependency — modules declare other modules and external libraries as flake inputs

docs.title: Build Pipeline
docs.text:
The builder orchestrates a multi-stage pipeline for each module:

metadata.json + src/
       │
       ▼
  ┌─────────────┐
  │ Parse Config │  parseMetadata.nix
  └──────┬──────┘
         │
         ▼
  ┌──────────────┐     ┌────────────────┐
  │ Build Ext.   │────▶│ External Libs  │  mkExternalLib.nix
  │ Libraries    │     │ (.a / .dylib)  │
  └──────┬───────┘     └───────┬────────┘
         │                     │
         ▼                     │
  ┌──────────────┐             │
  │ Build Plugin │◀────────────┘  backend.buildPlugin
  │ (.so/.dylib) │
  └──────┬───────┘
         │
    ┌────┴────┐
    │         │
    ▼         ▼
  ┌─────┐  ┌───────┐
  │ LGX │  │Headers│  backend.buildHeaders
  └─────┘  └───────┘

docs.title: Configuration as Single Source of Truth
docs.text:
metadata.json serves two purposes simultaneously:

  1. Build-time: The Nix build system reads it to determine dependencies, packages, external libraries, and CMake settings. These are under the nix key.
  2. Runtime: Qt's Q_PLUGIN_METADATA macro embeds it into the compiled plugin binary. The Logos runtime reads it for module name, version, type, dependencies, and capabilities.

This means a module author maintains one file, and both the build system and the runtime derive what they need from it.

docs.title: Backend Abstraction
docs.text:
The builder does not compile plugins directly. Instead, it delegates to a backend selected by the module's type field:

Module type Backend What it does core coreBackend Compiles a headless C++ plugin (logic-only, no UI) ui uiBackend Compiles a C++ Qt widget plugin with UI components ui_qml (built-in) No compilation — stages QML source files for runtime loading

Currently both coreBackend and uiBackend point to the same implementation (logos-plugin-qt), but they are separate flake inputs so coreBackend can be swapped to a non-Qt backend without affecting UI modules.

A backend must implement three functions: - buildPlugin { pkgs, src, config, ... } — compile the plugin shared library - buildHeaders { pkgs, src, config, pluginLib } — generate SDK headers from the compiled plugin - devShellInputs pkgs — provide dependencies for nix develop

docs.title: Module Dependencies
docs.text:
Modules declare dependencies on other modules by name in metadata.json:

{
  "dependencies": ["capability_module", "accounts_module"]
}

The builder automatically: 1. Matches dependency names to flake input names 2. Resolves each to its packages.<system>.default output 3. Copies include files from each dependency into generated_code/ so the module can use their SDK headers 4. For UI standalone apps: recursively collects all transitive dependencies and bundles them as LGX packages

docs.title: External Libraries
docs.text:
Modules that wrap third-party C/C++ libraries declare them in metadata.json:

{
  "nix": {
    "external_libraries": [
      {
        "name": "gowalletsdk",
        "build_command": "make static-library",
        "go_build": true,
        "output_pattern": "build/libgowalletsdk.*"
      }
    ]
  }
}

The builder: 1. Takes the library's source as a flake input (or vendor submodule) 2. Builds it in an isolated derivation with the appropriate toolchain (Go, Make, etc.) 3. Copies the resulting .a/.so/.dylib and headers into the module's build directory 4. The module's CMakeLists.txt links against the library using standard CMake

docs.title: Module Compilation
docs.text:
• C++17 Qt 6 plugin compilation via CMake and Ninja
  • Automatic Qt MOC (Meta-Object Compiler) processing
  • Platform-specific shared library output (.so on Linux, .dylib on macOS)
  • macOS install name and rpath fixups for plugin and all bundled libraries
  • dontStrip to preserve Qt plugin metadata sections in ELF/Mach-O binaries

docs.title: SDK Header Generation
docs.text:
• Automatic generation of type-safe C++ wrapper classes from compiled plugins
  • Uses logos-cpp-generator to introspect the plugin via QPluginLoader reflection
  • Produces per-module <name>_api.h/cpp and umbrella logos_sdk.h/cpp
  • Headers are installed to $out/include/ for downstream consumers

docs.title: LGX Packaging
docs.text:
• Every module automatically gets lgx and lgx-portable package outputs
  • Dev builds use platform-variant suffixes (e.g., linux-amd64-dev)
  • Portable builds use standard suffixes (e.g., linux-amd64)
  • Packages are created via nix-bundle-lgx

docs.title: Development Shells
docs.text:
• nix develop provides a complete build environment with Qt, CMake, Ninja, and all dependencies
  • Environment variables set: LOGOS_CPP_SDK_ROOT, LOGOS_MODULE_ROOT, LOGOS_MODULE_BUILDER_ROOT
  • Build-time and runtime packages from metadata.json are included automatically

docs.title: Standalone App Runner
docs.text:
• UI modules (type ui and ui_qml) automatically get nix run support
  • Creates a self-contained directory with the plugin, its transitive dependencies, and a host application
  • Dependencies are bundled as LGX packages, extracted at build time

docs.title: Template Scaffolding
docs.text: Four templates for nix flake init -t: - Minimal module — core logic plugin with basic structure - External library module — core plugin wrapping a third-party C/C++ library - UI module — C++ Qt widget plugin with createWidget()/destroyWidget() - QML module — Pure QML UI module with Main.qml

docs.title: Configuration Schema
docs.text:
The metadata.json schema includes:

Runtime fields (embedded in plugin): - name, version, type, category, description, main, icon - dependencies — other module names - include — additional files to bundle - capabilities — capabilities this module provides

Build fields (under nix key): - packages.build / packages.runtime — nix package names - external_libraries — external C/C++ library definitions - cmake.find_packages, cmake.extra_sources, cmake.extra_include_dirs, cmake.extra_link_libraries

docs.title: Supported Platforms
docs.text:
• macOS (aarch64-darwin, x86_64-darwin)
  • Linux (aarch64-linux, x86_64-linux)

docs.title: Future Work
docs.text:
• Non-Qt core backend — Allow core modules to compile without Qt dependency using a CBOR-based standalone backend
  • LIDL-based header generation — Generate SDK headers from .lidl interface definitions instead of requiring plugin introspection, removing the header generation dependency on the compiled plugin
  • Cross-compilation — Support building modules for platforms other than the host

docs.title: Tutorials: Wrapping an external library as a Logos module
docs.text:
These four tutorials each wrap the same trivial C library — libgreet, which exposes a single function:

const char* greet_hello(void);   // returns "hello from libgreet"

as a Logos core module whose hello() method forwards to greet_hello(). They differ only in where the library comes from and how the build obtains it:

# Tutorial The library is… Mechanism 1 Library source in the same repo .c/.h committed in your module compiled into the plugin via an in-repo CMake target 2 Prebuilt binaries in the same repo prebuilt .so/.dylib committed in your module vendor_path, with per-platform subdirectories 3 Source in an external repo (build with make) source in another repo flake input + build_command (e.g. make) 4 An external Nix flake a Nix flake exposing a package flake input, output used directly

Each tutorial is backed by a runnable doctest: a complete, buildable fixture under tests/fixtures/extlib-doctests/ that is built and checked in CI (see flake.nix checks.<system>.extlib-case*). The tutorials transclude their code straight from those fixtures, so the examples you read are exactly the sources that get compiled — they cannot drift. Every assertion in those checks also verifies the external symbol is actually linked, not silently dropped — so if a tutorial drifts from reality, CI fails.

docs.title: Which one do I want?
docs.text:
• You have the library's source and it builds with your module → Tutorial 1 (simplest; no extra inputs).
  • You only have prebuilt binaries (proprietary, slow to build, or cross-compiled elsewhere) → Tutorial 2. This is also the one to read for the platform disambiguation question — how to ship a linux x86_64 and a linux aarch64 build that both happen to be named libgreet.so.
  • The library lives in its own repo with a normal build system → Tutorial 3.
  • The library is already packaged as a Nix flake → Tutorial 4 (cleanest; the dependency is pinned and built by Nix, no build_command).

For the underlying reference (every external_libraries field, CMake hooks, platform/rpath handling), see the External Libraries Guide.

docs.title: Common ground
docs.text:
All four modules share the same shape (see any fixture's src/):

// <module>_plugin.cpp
#include "<module>_plugin.h"
#include "logos_api.h"
#include "lib/greet.h"          // the C library's header

QString <Module>Plugin::hello() {
    return QString::fromUtf8(greet_hello());   // call into the library
}

and are built, inspected, and run the same way:

ws build <module-repo>                       # nix build
lm methods ./result/lib/<name>_plugin.so     # list Q_INVOKABLE methods
logoscore -m ./result/lib -l <name> -c "<name>.hello()"

docs.title: Tutorial 1 — Library source in the same repo
docs.text:
Goal: wrap a C library whose source lives inside your module repo, compiling it directly into the plugin.

When to use: you have the library's .c/.h files and they build with a plain compiler. This is the simplest case — no flake inputs, no prebuilt binaries, no separate build step. The library becomes part of the plugin binary.

Runnable doctest

Every code block below is transcluded from the worked example at tests/fixtures/extlib-doctests/case1-source, which is built and checked in CI (checks.<system>.extlib-case1-source). The snippets you read here are the sources that get compiled — they cannot drift.

docs.title: Layout
docs.text:
greetsrc/
├── flake.nix
├── metadata.json
├── CMakeLists.txt
├── lib/
│   ├── greet.h          # the library's public header
│   └── greet.c          # the library's source
└── src/
    ├── greetsrc_interface.h
    ├── greetsrc_plugin.h
    └── greetsrc_plugin.cpp

docs.title: The library
metadata.json
docs.text:
No external_libraries entry is needed — the source is compiled in by CMake:

metadata.json
{
  "name": "greetsrc",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "type": "core",
  "category": "integration",
  "description": "Wraps a C library whose source (lib/greet.c) is committed in this repo",
  "main": "greetsrc_plugin",
  "dependencies": [],

  "nix": {
    "packages": {
      "build": [],
      "runtime": []
    },
    "external_libraries": [],
    "cmake": {
      "find_packages": [],
      "extra_sources": [],
      "extra_include_dirs": ["lib"],
      "extra_link_libraries": []
    }
  }
}

docs.title: flake.nix
CMakeLists.txt
docs.text:
Build the library source as a static target and link it into the plugin with LINK_TARGETS. POSITION_INDEPENDENT_CODE is required so the archive can be linked into the shared plugin; AUTOGEN_DEPENDS greet makes Qt's AUTOMOC wait for it:

CMakeLists.txt
# greetsrc — wraps a C library whose source is committed in this repo.
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.14)
project(GreetsrcModulePlugin LANGUAGES C CXX)

if(DEFINED ENV{LOGOS_MODULE_BUILDER_ROOT})
    include($ENV{LOGOS_MODULE_BUILDER_ROOT}/cmake/LogosModule.cmake)
elseif(EXISTS "${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/cmake/LogosModule.cmake")
    include(cmake/LogosModule.cmake)
else()
    message(FATAL_ERROR "LogosModule.cmake not found")
endif()

file(READ "${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/metadata.json" METADATA_JSON)
string(JSON MODULE_NAME GET ${METADATA_JSON} name)
string(JSON MODULE_VERSION GET ${METADATA_JSON} version)
file(WRITE "${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/module_config.h"
    "#pragma once\n"
    "#define MODULE_NAME \"${MODULE_NAME}\"\n"
    "#define MODULE_VERSION \"${MODULE_VERSION}\"\n"
)
include_directories("${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}")

# Build the in-repo library source as a static library. POSITION_INDEPENDENT_CODE
# is required so the archive can be linked into the shared plugin (ELF/Mach-O).
add_library(greet STATIC lib/greet.c)
set_target_properties(greet PROPERTIES POSITION_INDEPENDENT_CODE ON)
target_include_directories(greet PUBLIC "${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/lib")

# LINK_TARGETS links our static greet lib into the plugin; AUTOGEN_DEPENDS makes
# AUTOMOC wait for it. greet_hello() is compiled directly into the plugin binary.
logos_module(
    NAME ${MODULE_NAME}
    SOURCES
        src/greetsrc_interface.h
        src/greetsrc_plugin.h
        src/greetsrc_plugin.cpp
    LINK_TARGETS
        greet
    AUTOGEN_DEPENDS
        greet
)

Alternative

For a single source file you can skip the static-library target and just add lib/greet.c to SOURCES. The add_library form scales better to libraries with many sources and their own include tree.

docs.title: Plugin code
docs.text:
The interface declares the public API; the plugin calls greet_hello() through #include "lib/greet.h":

src/greetsrc_interface.h
#ifndef GREETSRC_INTERFACE_H
#define GREETSRC_INTERFACE_H

#include <QObject>
#include <QString>
#include "interface.h"

// Public API of the greetsrc module. hello() forwards to the wrapped C library's
// greet_hello() so the external symbol is load-bearing (a missing/unlinked
// library would surface as an unresolved symbol).
class GreetsrcInterface : public PluginInterface
{
public:
    virtual ~GreetsrcInterface() = default;

    Q_INVOKABLE virtual QString hello() = 0;
};

#define GreetsrcInterface_iid "org.logos.GreetsrcInterface"
Q_DECLARE_INTERFACE(GreetsrcInterface, GreetsrcInterface_iid)

#endif // GREETSRC_INTERFACE_H
src/greetsrc_plugin.h
#ifndef GREETSRC_PLUGIN_H
#define GREETSRC_PLUGIN_H

#include <QObject>
#include <QString>
#include "greetsrc_interface.h"
#include "module_config.h"

class LogosAPI;

// Compiles the library's source (lib/greet.c) straight into the plugin via an in-repo static library target.
class GreetsrcPlugin : public QObject, public GreetsrcInterface
{
    Q_OBJECT
    Q_PLUGIN_METADATA(IID GreetsrcInterface_iid FILE "metadata.json")
    Q_INTERFACES(GreetsrcInterface PluginInterface)

public:
    explicit GreetsrcPlugin(QObject* parent = nullptr);
    ~GreetsrcPlugin() override;

    QString name() const override { return MODULE_NAME; }
    QString version() const override { return MODULE_VERSION; }
    Q_INVOKABLE void initLogos(LogosAPI* api);

    Q_INVOKABLE QString hello() override;

signals:
    void eventResponse(const QString& eventName, const QVariantList& args);
};

#endif // GREETSRC_PLUGIN_H
src/greetsrc_plugin.cpp
#include "greetsrc_plugin.h"
#include "logos_api.h"
#include "lib/greet.h"
#include <QDebug>

GreetsrcPlugin::GreetsrcPlugin(QObject* parent)
    : QObject(parent)
{
}

GreetsrcPlugin::~GreetsrcPlugin() = default;

void GreetsrcPlugin::initLogos(LogosAPI* api)
{
    // Assign the inherited PluginInterface::logosAPI member — the host reads it
    // directly to dispatch cross-module calls.
    logosAPI = api;
}

QString GreetsrcPlugin::hello()
{
    const QString out = QString::fromUtf8(greet_hello());
    emit eventResponse("hello", QVariantList() << out);
    return out;
}

docs.title: Build, inspect, run
docs.text:
ws build greetsrc                                   # or: nix build
lm methods ./result/lib/greetsrc_plugin.so          # shows hello()
logoscore -m ./result/lib -l greetsrc -c "greetsrc.hello()"
# -> "hello from libgreet"

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Because the source is linked in, greet_hello is a defined symbol inside the plugin — there is no separate libgreet to ship. The doctest asserts this with nm (the symbol is present and not left undefined).
  • No RPATH/install_name handling is needed: nothing is loaded at runtime.

docs.title: Tutorial 2 — Prebuilt binaries in the same repo
docs.text:
Goal: wrap a library you only have as prebuilt binaries (.so/.dylib/ .a), committed in your module repo — and ship builds for several platforms at once.

When to use: the library is proprietary, slow to build, or produced by a separate toolchain (cross-compiled, vendor-supplied, CI artifact). You commit the binaries and the build links + bundles them.

Runnable doctest

The config/code blocks below are transcluded from the worked example at tests/fixtures/extlib-doctests/case2-binaries, which ships prebuilt libgreet for all four platforms and is built + checked in CI (checks.<system>.extlib-case2-binaries).

docs.title: The platform-disambiguation problem
docs.text: A single-platform vendored library is easy — drop it in lib/ and point vendor_path at it. But the moment you support more than one platform you hit a naming collision: linux x86_64 and linux aarch64 both use libgreet.so. They can't both sit in lib/.

docs.title: The solution: per-platform subdirectories
docs.text:
Put each platform's binary in a subdirectory named by its Nix system string. The build automatically selects the subdirectory matching the system it is building for; the shared header stays flat:

greetbin/
├── flake.nix
├── metadata.json
├── CMakeLists.txt
├── lib/
│   ├── greet.h                       # shared header, flat
│   ├── x86_64-linux/libgreet.so
│   ├── aarch64-linux/libgreet.so
│   ├── x86_64-darwin/libgreet.dylib
│   └── aarch64-darwin/libgreet.dylib
└── src/
    ├── greetbin_interface.h
    ├── greetbin_plugin.h
    └── greetbin_plugin.cpp

The four valid subdirectory names are exactly the Nix systems Logos targets:

Subdirectory Platform x86_64-linux Linux, Intel/AMD 64-bit aarch64-linux Linux, ARM 64-bit x86_64-darwin macOS, Intel aarch64-darwin macOS, Apple Silicon

These are Nix system strings

They are what nix eval --raw --expr builtins.currentSystem prints — not the .lgx packaging variant labels (linux-amd64, darwin-arm64). Don't confuse the two.

You only need to commit the platforms you actually support. A flat lib/libgreet.<ext> (no subdir) is the single-platform form and keeps working unchanged — just don't mix the two layouts for the same library.

docs.title: metadata.json
docs.text:
The external_libraries entry is the same as for a single-platform vendored lib — vendor_path points at the directory holding the per-platform subdirs:

metadata.json
{
  "name": "greetbin",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "type": "core",
  "category": "integration",
  "description": "Wraps a prebuilt libgreet binary vendored per-platform under lib/<nix-system>/",
  "main": "greetbin_plugin",
  "dependencies": [],

  "nix": {
    "packages": {
      "build": [],
      "runtime": []
    },
    "external_libraries": [
      { "name": "greet", "vendor_path": "lib" }
    ],
    "cmake": {
      "find_packages": [],
      "extra_sources": [],
      "extra_include_dirs": ["lib"],
      "extra_link_libraries": []
    }
  }
}

docs.title: flake.nix
docs.text:
No externalLibInputs — the binaries are in the repo:

flake.nix
{
  description = "greetbin — Logos module wrapping a per-platform prebuilt libgreet binary";

  inputs = {
    logos-module-builder.url = "github:logos-co/logos-module-builder";
  };

  outputs = inputs@{ logos-module-builder, ... }:
    logos-module-builder.lib.mkLogosModule {
      src = ./.;
      configFile = ./metadata.json;
      flakeInputs = inputs;
      # No externalLibInputs: the library is a prebuilt binary committed under
      # lib/<nix-system>/ and selected automatically by the build.
    };
}

docs.title: CMakeLists.txt
docs.text:
EXTERNAL_LIBS greet finds, links, adds lib/ as an include dir, and copies the shared library next to the plugin:

CMakeLists.txt
# greetbin — wraps a prebuilt libgreet binary vendored per-platform.
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.14)
project(GreetbinModulePlugin LANGUAGES CXX)

# Include the Logos Module CMake helper (provided via LOGOS_MODULE_BUILDER_ROOT
# during a nix build).
if(DEFINED ENV{LOGOS_MODULE_BUILDER_ROOT})
    include($ENV{LOGOS_MODULE_BUILDER_ROOT}/cmake/LogosModule.cmake)
elseif(EXISTS "${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/cmake/LogosModule.cmake")
    include(cmake/LogosModule.cmake)
else()
    message(FATAL_ERROR "LogosModule.cmake not found")
endif()

# Derive module identity from metadata.json
file(READ "${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/metadata.json" METADATA_JSON)
string(JSON MODULE_NAME GET ${METADATA_JSON} name)
string(JSON MODULE_VERSION GET ${METADATA_JSON} version)
file(WRITE "${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/module_config.h"
    "#pragma once\n"
    "#define MODULE_NAME \"${MODULE_NAME}\"\n"
    "#define MODULE_VERSION \"${MODULE_VERSION}\"\n"
)
include_directories("${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}")

# EXTERNAL_LIBS greet => find_library(libgreet) in lib/, link it, add lib/ as an
# include dir, and copy the shared lib next to the plugin. The builder stages the
# platform-matching binary from lib/<nix-system>/ into lib/ before this runs.
logos_module(
    NAME ${MODULE_NAME}
    SOURCES
        src/greetbin_interface.h
        src/greetbin_plugin.h
        src/greetbin_plugin.cpp
    EXTERNAL_LIBS
        greet
)

docs.title: Plugin code
docs.text:
hello() calls greet_hello() from the vendored binary via the flat header:

lib/greet.h
#ifndef GREET_H
#define GREET_H

#ifdef __cplusplus
extern "C" {
#endif

/* Returns a static greeting string owned by the library. */
const char* greet_hello(void);

#ifdef __cplusplus
}
#endif

#endif /* GREET_H */
src/greetbin_plugin.cpp
#include "greetbin_plugin.h"
#include "logos_api.h"
#include "lib/greet.h"
#include <QDebug>

GreetbinPlugin::GreetbinPlugin(QObject* parent)
    : QObject(parent)
{
}

GreetbinPlugin::~GreetbinPlugin() = default;

void GreetbinPlugin::initLogos(LogosAPI* api)
{
    // Assign the inherited PluginInterface::logosAPI member — the host reads it
    // directly to dispatch cross-module calls.
    logosAPI = api;
}

QString GreetbinPlugin::hello()
{
    // Calls into the vendored libgreet binary. If the wrong-arch binary were
    // selected (or none), this symbol would be unresolved at load time.
    const QString out = QString::fromUtf8(greet_hello());
    emit eventResponse("hello", QVariantList() << out);
    return out;
}

(The full greetbin_interface.h / greetbin_plugin.h are in the fixture.)

docs.title: How selection works
docs.text:
git add your binaries (Nix only sees git-tracked files), then build:

ws build greetbin                                  # or: nix build
lm methods ./result/lib/greetbin_plugin.so
logoscore -m ./result/lib -l greetbin -c "greetbin.hello()"
# -> "hello from libgreet"

During the build, mkExternalLib detects the lib/<nix-system>/ subdir for the build platform and stages that one binary into lib/, where CMake's find_library picks it up. The bundled libgreet.<ext> is placed next to the plugin and the RPATH (@loader_path on macOS, $ORIGIN on Linux) resolves it at runtime. On macOS, install_name/@rpath are fixed up automatically.

docs.title: Producing the binaries
docs.text:
Build libgreet for each platform with your normal toolchain (cc -shared -fPIC -o libgreet.so greet.c), or cross-compile all four with zig (handy for ELF + Mach-O from one host):

zig cc -target x86_64-linux-gnu  -shared -fPIC -Wl,-soname,libgreet.so      -o lib/x86_64-linux/libgreet.so      greet.c
zig cc -target aarch64-linux-gnu -shared -fPIC -Wl,-soname,libgreet.so      -o lib/aarch64-linux/libgreet.so     greet.c
zig cc -target x86_64-macos  -shared -fPIC -Wl,-install_name,@rpath/libgreet.dylib -o lib/x86_64-darwin/libgreet.dylib  greet.c
zig cc -target aarch64-macos -shared -fPIC -Wl,-install_name,@rpath/libgreet.dylib -o lib/aarch64-darwin/libgreet.dylib greet.c

Give shared libraries a SONAME (Linux) / install_name (macOS) of the bare filename (libgreet.so / @rpath/libgreet.dylib) so the plugin records a relocatable dependency that the RPATH can resolve. A vendored .a static archive is linked in at build time and needs neither.

docs.title: Notes & limitations
docs.text:
• The doctest verifies the correct architecture was selected (file on the staged binary) and that the plugin genuinely links it (otool -L / readelf -d showing the dependency) — important on macOS, where -undefined dynamic_lookup would otherwise let a missing library link silently.
  • Raw nix develop + cmake does not descend into lib/<nix-system>/; the subdir selection happens during the Nix build's staging step. Build with ws build / nix build (the supported path). For raw-cmake iteration, copy the right subdir's binary into lib/ yourself.

docs.title: Tutorial 3 — Source in an external repo (build with make)
docs.text:
Goal: wrap a library whose source lives in a separate repository with its own build system, pinned as a flake input and built during your module's Nix build.

When to use: the library is maintained elsewhere, you want its source pinned (reproducible) rather than vendoring binaries, and it builds with a normal command (make, ./build.sh, a cmake invocation, …).

Runnable doctest

The blocks below are transcluded from the worked example at tests/fixtures/extlib-doctests/case3-external-source, built + checked in CI (checks.<system>.extlib-case3-external-source). The greet-src/ directory stands in for the external library's repo.

docs.title: Layout
docs.text:
greetmake/
├── flake.nix
├── metadata.json
├── CMakeLists.txt
└── src/
    ├── greetmake_interface.h
    ├── greetmake_plugin.h
    └── greetmake_plugin.cpp

The library source is not in your repo — it comes from the flake input. The external repo just needs a build that emits a shared library. Here is the stand-in external repo's Makefile ($(CC) comes from the Nix stdenv — clang on macOS, gcc on Linux):

greet-src/Makefile (the external repo)
# Minimal Makefile for libgreet — stands in for an external library's build
# system. The Logos builder runs `make` (the metadata build_command) in the
# nix sandbox; $(CC) comes from the stdenv (clang on Darwin, gcc on Linux).
CC ?= cc
UNAME_S := $(shell uname -s)

ifeq ($(UNAME_S),Darwin)
  EXT := dylib
  SOFLAGS := -dynamiclib -install_name @rpath/libgreet.dylib
else
  EXT := so
  SOFLAGS := -shared -Wl,-soname,libgreet.so
endif

.PHONY: all
all: build/libgreet.$(EXT)

build/libgreet.$(EXT): greet.c greet.h
    mkdir -p build
    $(CC) -fPIC $(SOFLAGS) -o build/libgreet.$(EXT) greet.c

.PHONY: clean
clean:
    rm -rf build

docs.title: metadata.json
docs.text:
Declare the library with a build_command and where its output lands:

metadata.json
{
  "name": "greetmake",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "type": "core",
  "category": "integration",
  "description": "Wraps a C library whose source lives in an external repo, built with make",
  "main": "greetmake_plugin",
  "dependencies": [],

  "nix": {
    "packages": {
      "build": [],
      "runtime": []
    },
    "external_libraries": [
      {
        "name": "greet",
        "build_command": "make",
        "output_pattern": "build/libgreet.*"
      }
    ],
    "cmake": {
      "find_packages": [],
      "extra_sources": [],
      "extra_include_dirs": ["lib"],
      "extra_link_libraries": []
    }
  }
}
  • build_command — run in the unpacked source (default make). Examples: "make shared", "./build.sh", "mkdir build && cd build && cmake .. && make".
  • output_pattern — where the build leaves the library (default build/lib<name>.*). The builder also probes common locations and copies any *.h it finds into the include set.
  • For Go libraries that emit a C archive, add "go_build": true and a "vendor_hash" (see the External Libraries Guide).

docs.title: flake.nix
docs.text:
Add the source repo as a non-flake input and pass it via externalLibInputs. Because it is raw source (not a Nix derivation), the builder runs the build_command:

flake.nix
{
  description = "greetmake — Logos module wrapping an external library source built with make";

  inputs = {
    logos-module-builder.url = "github:logos-co/logos-module-builder";

    # The external library's source repo, pinned as a non-flake input.
    greet = {
      url = "github:example/libgreet";
      flake = false;
    };
  };

  outputs = inputs@{ logos-module-builder, ... }:
    logos-module-builder.lib.mkLogosModule {
      src = ./.;
      configFile = ./metadata.json;
      flakeInputs = inputs;
      # Pass the source repo as an external library input. Because it is a
      # non-flake source, the builder runs the metadata `build_command` (make).
      externalLibInputs = {
        greet = inputs.greet;
      };
    };
}

The attribute name in externalLibInputs (greet) must match the name in the external_libraries entry.

docs.title: CMakeLists.txt
docs.text:
EXTERNAL_LIBS greet finds, links, and bundles the built library — the builder has already staged libgreet.<ext> and greet.h into lib/:

CMakeLists.txt
# greetmake — wraps a library whose source is built from an external repo with make.
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.14)
project(GreetmakeModulePlugin LANGUAGES CXX)

if(DEFINED ENV{LOGOS_MODULE_BUILDER_ROOT})
    include($ENV{LOGOS_MODULE_BUILDER_ROOT}/cmake/LogosModule.cmake)
elseif(EXISTS "${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/cmake/LogosModule.cmake")
    include(cmake/LogosModule.cmake)
else()
    message(FATAL_ERROR "LogosModule.cmake not found")
endif()

file(READ "${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/metadata.json" METADATA_JSON)
string(JSON MODULE_NAME GET ${METADATA_JSON} name)
string(JSON MODULE_VERSION GET ${METADATA_JSON} version)
file(WRITE "${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/module_config.h"
    "#pragma once\n"
    "#define MODULE_NAME \"${MODULE_NAME}\"\n"
    "#define MODULE_VERSION \"${MODULE_VERSION}\"\n"
)
include_directories("${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}")

# The builder runs `make` on the external source, then stages libgreet + greet.h
# into lib/. EXTERNAL_LIBS greet finds, links, and bundles it.
logos_module(
    NAME ${MODULE_NAME}
    SOURCES
        src/greetmake_interface.h
        src/greetmake_plugin.h
        src/greetmake_plugin.cpp
    EXTERNAL_LIBS
        greet
)

docs.title: Plugin code
docs.text:
Same shape as the other tutorials — hello() calls greet_hello() via #include "lib/greet.h" (the header was staged from the external build's output):

src/greetmake_plugin.cpp

#include "logos_api.h"
#include "lib/greet.h"
#include <QDebug>

{
}

    // Assign the inherited PluginInterface::logosAPI member — the host reads it
    // directly to dispatch cross-module calls.

    const QString out = QString::fromUtf8(greet_hello());

docs.title: Build, inspect, run
docs.text:
ws build greetmake                                  # or: nix build
lm methods ./result/lib/greetmake_plugin.so
logoscore -m ./result/lib -l greetmake -c "greetmake.hello()"
# -> "hello from libgreet"

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The build runs in the Nix sandbox with gnumake + pkg-config available. If your build_command needs more tools, add them to nix.packages.build.
  • The doctest builds the external source with make, then asserts the resulting libgreet is bundled next to the plugin and actually linked (otool -L / readelf -d).
  • If the external library is already packaged as a Nix flake, prefer Tutorial 4 — no build_command needed.

docs.title: Tutorial 4 — An external Nix flake
docs.text:
Goal: wrap a library that is already packaged as a Nix flake exposing a package output (a derivation with lib/ + include/). The builder uses that output directly — no build_command.

When to use: the library has its own flake.nix that builds it with Nix. This is the cleanest option: the dependency is pinned in your flake.lock, built reproducibly by Nix, and you choose exactly which output to consume.

Runnable doctest

The module's blocks below are transcluded from the worked example at tests/fixtures/extlib-doctests/case4-flake-package, built + checked in CI (checks.<system>.extlib-case4-flake-package). The check stands in for the external flake by building libgreet as a derivation and selecting it through the same structured externalLibInputs form shown below.

docs.title: How the builder decides
docs.text: When you point externalLibInputs at a resolved flake package (a Nix derivation), the builder detects it (lib.isDerivation) and uses it as-is — it copies the derivation's lib/ and include/ into your module. If you instead point it at raw source (flake = false), it runs the build_command (Tutorial 3). Same field, two behaviors, chosen by what the input resolves to.

docs.title: What the external flake looks like
docs.text:
The library's flake just needs to expose a package per system with lib/ and include/ (illustrative — substitute your real library flake):

# flake.nix of the external libgreet
outputs = { self, nixpkgs, ... }: let
  forAll = f: nixpkgs.lib.genAttrs
    [ "x86_64-linux" "aarch64-linux" "x86_64-darwin" "aarch64-darwin" ]
    (system: f (import nixpkgs { inherit system; }));
in {
  packages = forAll (pkgs: {
    lib = pkgs.stdenv.mkDerivation {
      pname = "libgreet"; version = "1.0.0";
      src = ./.;
      installPhase = ''
        mkdir -p $out/lib $out/include
        $CC -fPIC -shared -Wl,-soname,libgreet.so -o $out/lib/libgreet.so greet.c
        cp greet.h $out/include/
      '';
    };
    default = self.packages.${pkgs.system}.lib;
  });
};

docs.title: metadata.json
docs.text:
Only the name is needed — there is no build_command, because the input is already built:

metadata.json
{
  "name": "greetflake",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "type": "core",
  "category": "integration",
  "description": "Wraps a C library provided by an external Nix flake (package output used directly)",
  "main": "greetflake_plugin",
  "dependencies": [],

  "nix": {
    "packages": {
      "build": [],
      "runtime": []
    },
    "external_libraries": [
      { "name": "greet" }
    ],
    "cmake": {
      "find_packages": [],
      "extra_sources": [],
      "extra_include_dirs": ["lib"],
      "extra_link_libraries": []
    }
  }
}

docs.title: flake.nix
docs.text:
Add the library flake as an input and select the output with the structured externalLibInputs form (input + packages):

flake.nix
{
  description = "greetflake — Logos module wrapping a library from an external Nix flake";

  inputs = {
    logos-module-builder.url = "github:logos-co/logos-module-builder";

    # The external library, provided as a Nix flake that exposes
    # packages.<system>.lib (a derivation with lib/ + include/).
    greet.url = "github:example/libgreet-flake";
  };

  outputs = inputs@{ logos-module-builder, ... }:
    logos-module-builder.lib.mkLogosModule {
      src = ./.;
      configFile = ./metadata.json;
      flakeInputs = inputs;
      # Structured form: pick which package output of the flake to use. Because
      # the resolved value is a Nix derivation, the builder uses it directly —
      # no build_command needed.
      externalLibInputs = {
        greet = {
          input = inputs.greet;
          packages.default = "lib";
        };
      };
    };
}

packages.default = "lib" tells the builder to use greet.packages.<system>.lib. If the flake exposes multiple builds (e.g. a portable variant), map them per-variant — packages = { default = "lib"; portable = "lib-portable"; }. If packages.<system>.default is what you want, the shorthand externalLibInputs = { greet = inputs.greet; } also works.

docs.title: CMakeLists.txt
docs.text:
EXTERNAL_LIBS greet finds, links, and bundles the flake-provided library — the builder has staged its lib/ + include/ into lib/:

CMakeLists.txt
# greetflake — wraps a library provided by an external Nix flake.
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.14)
project(GreetflakeModulePlugin LANGUAGES CXX)

if(DEFINED ENV{LOGOS_MODULE_BUILDER_ROOT})
    include($ENV{LOGOS_MODULE_BUILDER_ROOT}/cmake/LogosModule.cmake)
elseif(EXISTS "${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/cmake/LogosModule.cmake")
    include(cmake/LogosModule.cmake)
else()
    message(FATAL_ERROR "LogosModule.cmake not found")
endif()

file(READ "${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/metadata.json" METADATA_JSON)
string(JSON MODULE_NAME GET ${METADATA_JSON} name)
string(JSON MODULE_VERSION GET ${METADATA_JSON} version)
file(WRITE "${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/module_config.h"
    "#pragma once\n"
    "#define MODULE_NAME \"${MODULE_NAME}\"\n"
    "#define MODULE_VERSION \"${MODULE_VERSION}\"\n"
)
include_directories("${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}")

# The flake's package output (lib/ + include/) is staged into lib/ by the
# builder. EXTERNAL_LIBS greet finds, links, and bundles it.
logos_module(
    NAME ${MODULE_NAME}
    SOURCES
        src/greetflake_interface.h
        src/greetflake_plugin.h
        src/greetflake_plugin.cpp
    EXTERNAL_LIBS
        greet
)

Same shape as the other tutorials — hello() calls greet_hello() via #include "lib/greet.h":

docs.title: Build, inspect, run
docs.text:
ws build greetflake                                  # or: nix build
lm methods ./result/lib/greetflake_plugin.so

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The doctest builds libgreet as a Nix derivation, exposes it as packages.<system>.greetlib, and selects it with { input = …; packages.default = "greetlib"; } — exactly the structured form above. It then asserts the flake-built library is bundled and linked (otool -L / readelf -d).
  • Because Nix builds the dependency, its transitive runtime libraries are handled by the closure; the RPATH (@loader_path/$ORIGIN) resolves the bundled libgreet next to the plugin.
